sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
सन्न्यासः कर्मयोगश्च निःश्रेयसकरावुभौ ।
तयोस्तु कर्मसन्न्यासात्कर्मयोगो विशिष्यते ॥ २ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
sannyāsaḥ karma-yogaś ca niḥśreyasa-karāv ubhau |
tayos tu karma-sannyāsāt karma-yogo viśiṣyate || 2 ||

anvaya.meaning: lead to the supreme good / liberation
is superior / is distinguished as better

translations.verbatim_quote: The Personality of Godhead replied: The renunciation of work and work in devotion are both good for liberation. But, of the two, work in devotional service is better than renunciation of work.
The Personality of Godhead replied: The renunciation of work and work in devotion are both good for liberation. But, of the two, work in devotional service is better than renunciation of work.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 3405 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Fruitive activities (seeking sense gratification) are cause for material bondage. As long as one is engaged in activities aimed at improving the standard of bodily comfort, one is sure to transmigrate to different types of bodies, thereby continuing material bondage perpetually.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.2 Ubhau, both, to be sure; sannyasah, renunciation of actions; ca, and; karma-yogah, Karma-yoga-their performance-; nihsreyasa-karau, lead to Liberation. Though both lead to Liberation by virtue of being the cause of the rise of Knowledge, even then, tayoh, between the two which are the causes of…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-2-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara stresses the two-half structure: both sannyāsa and karma-yoga lead to liberation (because both can give rise to Knowledge); however, of these two, karma-yoga is to be regarded as superior to the renunciation of action. He glosses 'tu' and 'viśiṣyate' carefully — the preference is structural, not absolute. Karma-yoga is praised here in the context of the unenlightened person who has not yet realized non-doership; for such a person, karma-yoga is the appropriate practice, while pure sannyāsa belongs to the one already established in jñāna-niṣṭhā.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.2 The Lord said — Even while granting that some persons are competent for the practice of Jnana Yoga exclusively, it has to be conceded that renunciation, i.e., Jnana Yoga, and Karma Yoga can be practised as independent of each other in the pursuit of the highest excellence.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-2-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: even granting that some are competent for the practice of jñāna-yoga (which he equates with sannyāsa here) exclusively, both sannyāsa and karma-yoga are independent means leading to the supreme good. Of these two, karma-yoga is the superior. This is a distinct claim from Shankara's: for Ramanuja the two are independent, not stages of a single ladder, and the preference of karma-yoga is direct.

positions.rendering: is superior, but staged: karma-yoga for the unenlightened, sannyāsa for the established jñānin

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Shankara reads the preference as staged (karma-yoga is superior *for the still-becoming aspirant*); Ramanuja reads the preference as direct (karma-yoga is superior simpliciter, given that both are valid independent means). Prabhupada flattens this into 'better' without distinguishing the staged-vs-direct disagreement.

literal_meaning: The Supreme Lord said: Both renunciation of action and karma-yoga lead to the supreme good. Of these two, however, karma-yoga is superior to the renunciation of action.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna gives the chapter's thesis-verdict in two halves. First: both paths produce niḥśreyas (the supreme good, liberation). Second: of the two, karma-yoga is to be regarded as superior. The two halves are load-bearing and must be preserved together. This is not a flat 'either is fine' equivalence; nor is it 'sannyāsa is wrong'. It is: equivalence-of-destination plus structural-preference-in-practice.
